title: Rational equations intro
description: Solve equations that have one rational expression whose numerator and denominator are linear expressions.
topic_path: Module 1: Relationships between quantities and reasoning with equations and their graphs

hints:
First, we note that $k=0$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $3k$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{5k - 2}{3k} &= 7
\\\\
(3k)\cdot \dfrac{5k - 2}{3k} &= (3k)\cdot 7
\\\\
5k-2&=21k
\\\\
-2&=16k
\\\\
-\dfrac18&=k
\end{align}$

We get that $k=-\dfrac18$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**In conclusion, $k = -\dfrac{1}{8}$.**

First, we note that $r=7$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $r-7$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{4r - 6}{r - 7} &= \dfrac{1}{2}
\\\\
(r-7)\cdot \dfrac{4r - 6}{r - 7} &= (r-7)\cdot \dfrac{1}{2}
\\\\
4r-6&=\dfrac12 r-\dfrac72
\\\\
\dfrac72 r&=\dfrac52
\\\\
r&=\dfrac57
\end{align}$

We get that $r=\dfrac57$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**In conclusion, $r = \dfrac{5}{7}$.**

First, we note that $x=-\dfrac13$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $3x+1$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{3x - 2}{3x + 1} &= \dfrac{1}{3}
\\\\
(3x+1)\cdot \dfrac{3x - 2}{3x + 1} &= (3x+1)\cdot \dfrac{1}{3}
\\\\
3x-2&=x+\dfrac13
\\\\
2x&=\dfrac73
\\\\
x&=\dfrac76
\end{align}$

We get that $x=\dfrac76$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**In conclusion, $x=\dfrac76$.**

First, we note that $p=-\dfrac65$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $5p+6$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{2p + 1}{5p + 6} &= \dfrac{1}{7}
\\\\
(5p+6)\cdot \dfrac{2p + 1}{5p + 6} &= (5p+6)\cdot \dfrac{1}{7}
\\\\
2p+1&=\dfrac57 p+\dfrac67
\\\\
\dfrac97 p&=-\dfrac17
\\\\
p&=-\dfrac19
\end{align}$

We get that $p=-\dfrac19$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**$p = -\dfrac19$**

First, we note that $k=3$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $2k-6$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{6}{2k - 6} &= \dfrac{1}{3}
\\\\
(2k-6)\cdot \dfrac{6}{2k - 6} &=(2k-6)\cdot \dfrac{1}{3}
\\\\
6&=\dfrac23 k-2
\\\\
8&=\dfrac23 k
\\\\
12&=k
\end{align}$

We get that $k=12$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**In conclusion, $k=12$.**

First, we note that $q=-\dfrac32$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $2q+3$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{2}{2q + 3} &= \dfrac{1}{8}
\\\\
(2q+3)\cdot \dfrac{2}{2q + 3} &= (2q+3)\cdot \dfrac{1}{8}
\\\\
2&=\dfrac28 q+\dfrac38
\\\\
\dfrac{13}{8}&=\dfrac28q
\\\\
\dfrac{13}{2}&=q
\end{align}$

We get that $q=\dfrac{13}{2}$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**In conclusion, $q = \dfrac{13}{2}$.**

First, we note that $k=0$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $2k$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{3}{2k} &= 5
\\\\
(2k)\cdot \dfrac{3}{2k} &= (2k)\cdot 5
\\\\
3&=10k
\\\\
\dfrac{3}{10}&=k
\end{align}$

We get that $k=\dfrac{3}{10}$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**$k=\dfrac{3}{10}$**

First, we note that $q=-4$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $2q+8$ to get rid of the quotient.

First, we note that $q=-4$ makes the denominator zero and so cannot be a solution.

Now, we multiply both sides of the equation by $2q+8$ to get rid of the quotient.

$\begin{align}
\dfrac{10}{2q + 8} &= \dfrac{1}{10}
\\\\
(2q+8)\cdot \dfrac{10}{2q + 8} &= (2q+8)\cdot \dfrac{1}{10}
\\\\
10&=\dfrac15 q+\dfrac45
\\\\
\dfrac{46}{5}&=\dfrac15 q
\\\\
46&=q
\end{align}$

We get that $q=46$ is a potential solution. It doesn't make the denominator zero, so it is indeed a solution.

**In conclusion, $q=46$.**
